coredata.dc:title: First-principles investigations on the feasibility of the GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Feng-Lin
Zhang, Zhi-Hong
Yang, Chuan-Lu
Wang, Mei-Shan
Ma, Xiao-Guang
coredata.dc:description: The feasibility of nanocomposites of phycourobilin (PUB) and phycoerythrobilin (PEB) with graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) sensitizer is studied based on the first-principles density functional theory including solvent effect. We construct and optimize the geometrical configurations for the nanocomposites of GQD-PEB/PUB and confirm the stability by the vibrational frequency and formation energy. The results of electronic properties show that the GQD2-PEB nanocomposite could be a favorable candidate for the sensitizer of DSSC due to its apparent spatial charge separation, suitable energies of frontier orbital, and enhanced absorption coefficient in the large solar irradiance region.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Graphene quantum dots
Phycourobilin and phycoerythrobilin
Sensitizer
Optical absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The geometrical structures and stabilities of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites
Electron transfer between the components of nanocomposite
Optical absorptions of the GQD, PEB/PUB, and their nanocomposites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant Nos. NSFC-11874192).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structures of the separate GQD, PEB, PUB and GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites.
The vibrational spectra of GQD and GQD-PEB/PUB at B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level. The abscissa represents harmonic frequency in cm−1, while the ordinate is absorption intensity in a.u.
The HOMOs and LUMOs of the considered structures at B3LYP/6–31 + G(d). The redox potential of I−/I3 − electrolyte and the CBM of TiO2 are included as the reference points.
The charge densities of the HOMOs and LUMOs for the GQD-PEB and GQD-PUB nanocomposites.
The optical absorptions for all the considered structures at the TD-DFT/B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) with PCM of water. The longitudinal axis is molar absorption coefficient.

The calculated E 0-0,
E
ox
na
n
∗
, ΔGinject , LHE and Voc for all the considered structures.

head.ce:title: First-principles investigations on the feasibility of the GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The feasibility of nanocomposites of phycourobilin (PUB) and phycoerythrobilin (PEB) with graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) sensitizer is studied based on the first-principles density functional theory including solvent effect. We construct and optimize the geometrical configurations for the nanocomposites of GQD-PEB/PUB and confirm the stability by the vibrational frequency and formation energy. The results of electronic properties show that the GQD2-PEB nanocomposite could be a favorable candidate for the sensitizer of DSSC due to its apparent spatial charge separation, suitable energies of frontier orbital, and enhanced absorption coefficient in the large solar irradiance region.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy is indispensable for the living and development of human beings. Since the widespread use of steam engines, non-renewable energy sources like oil, coal, and gas have been consumed at geometric indices. At the same time, the burning of fossil fuels is also an important cause of global warming. Therefore, in order to achieve the goal of global carbon neutralization, the development of renewable and environmentally friendly energy has become a growing concern. The sun provides the most energy available to date, and the energy shining on Earth in an hour (4.3 × 1020 J) is more than all energy consumed by the human in a year (4.1 × 1020 J) [1]. Therefore, the usage of solar energy is much interested in researchers. One of the promising ways is to convert solar energy directly into electricity through photovoltaic devices. At present, the main photovoltaic solar cells on the market are mainly doped with single crystalline silicon or polysilicon [2], and some cadmium telluride, cadmium sulfide [3,4]. However, due to the tedious process of extraction, recovery, and purification, the cost is high, and rare elements such as telluride and cadmium are rare and toxic. Therefore, it is still an urgent task to find effective and safe materials to realize photovoltaic conversion. So, the dye-sensitized solar cells(DSSC) have entered into the researcher's sight because of the low cost, easy manufacture [5,6], and integration into the building structures [7,8].

In 1991, Grätzel et al. [9] proposed a DSSC with a photoelectric conversion efficiency of 7.1%, raising a new upsurge to improve DSSC. All the dye sensitizers [10], photoanodes [11], and electrolytes [12] are paid attention to. The dyes are mainly divided into organic metal dyes [13], Metal-free organic dyes [14], and natural dye. The natural dye opened the door to low-cost and non-toxic alternatives to synthetic sensitizers, but most natural dyes are extracted [15] from higher plants that grow slowly and are greatly affected by the environment [16]. In contrast, the more efficient way is to extract pigments from algae, fungi, etc. These organisms are less demanding of the environment, and people can control their growth rate in a given environment. However, the effects of many natural dyes are unsatisfactory because of the photo-degradation in the sunlight, making the solar cell photoelectric conversion efficiency even less than 1% [17]. Therefore, in order to make natural dyes become efficient photosensitizers, researchers improved the natural dyes by modifying the structure with some functional ligands or quantum dots [18,19].

The nanocomposite sensitizers constructed with dye and quantum dots (QD) have been proved to be a useful way to promote the efficiency of DSSC [20] because nanocomposites can demonstrate excellent optical absorption properties compared with each component. For example, Jiang et al. [21] used the CdSeTe QDs as a sensitizer and explored hierarchically-assembled indium tin oxide (ITO)/Cu2S nanowire arrays counter electrode to increase the photoelectric conversion efficiency to 6.12%. Moreover, graphene quantum dots (GQD) has been paid much attention to because of the adjustable size and bandgap, good chemical stability, and free of environmental pollution. Mandal et al. [22] introduced the theoretical results of the porphyrin functionalized GQD, pointing out the feasibility of the porphyrin-GQD nanocomposite as a DSSC sensitizer. In 2019, Gao et al. [23] reported that the use of nanocomposites formed using graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD) and Tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP) became a promising sensitizer and provided an effective reference for designing nanocomposites to harvest solar energy.

Recently, the Phycourobilin (PUB) and Phycoerythrobilin (PEB) have been gained attention as the auxiliary photosynthesis pigments in red algae and cyanobacteria. Similar to chlorophyll, they are also connected by four pyrrole rings through methylene with straight chains but without magnesium atoms. Since the two dyes have absorptions in the visible light range but are not strong, we consider enhancing the absorption by forming nanocomposites with GQDs. By employing the density functional theory (DFT), we optimize the geometrical configurations of the nanocomposites and assure the stabilities by analysis of frequency and formation energy. The time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) is used to calculate the optical absorption. The results show that the optical absorptions of GQD2-PEB and GQD2-PUB are about 5 times that of PEB/PUB in the range from 400 to 600 nm. Meanwhile, the spatial charge separation of the nanocomposites shows that the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQD1-PEB and GQD2-PEB are located at the PEB and GQD if the nanocomposites are formed properly. The obvious spatial charge separation can effectively inhibit the electron recombination rate and thus improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency. The large open-circuit voltage of 1.74 V and the high light-harvesting efficiency of 0.997 are observed for GQD2-PEB, which suggests that the newly constructed nanocomposites could be potential candidates as the sensitizer of DSSC.

Three graphene quantum dots cut from a large graphene sheet are considered. Hereafter, they are represented with GQD1 (C32H14), GQD2 (C54H18), and GQD3 (C66H20), respectively. The GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites are constructed by connecting the PEB or PUB molecules to GQD with an amide linkage, as shown in Fig. 1 . All the calculations for the electronic properties were completed by using the hybrid density functional theory(B3LYP) [24,25] method with the basis sets of 6–31 + G(d) [26] implemented in the Gaussian 16 program [27]. The harmonic frequencies and formation energy of all structures are calculated at the same theoretical level as the geometrical optimizations to evaluate the energy stability of the nanocomposites. The optimized structures of the GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites are subjected to calculate the oscillator strength by using TDDFT. We used the polarized continuum model (PCM) [28,29] to investigate the solvent effect (in water) because dye-sensitizer often work in the solvent containing I-/I3 - in DSSC. The default convergent criteria of energy and force constants in Gaussian 16 are adopted in the DFT and TDDFT calculations. The liability of TDDFT is assured by comparing the absorption peak of PEB/PUB by TDDFT with the corresponding experimental values [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The geometrical structures and stabilities of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
The obtained geometrical structures of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites after optimization calculations are shown in Fig. 1. The harmonic frequency range in Table 1 and the detailed vibrational spectra in Fig. 2 indicate that no negative value is found for the structures, confirming that all the considered nanocomposites are energetically stable. The formation energy Ef of the nanocomposite is determined by
(1)
E f
=
E nanocomposite
-
E
G Q D
-
E
P E B / P U B
where E nanocomposite, E GQD, and E PEB/PUB are the total energies of the nanocomposite, GQD, and PEB/PUB, respectively. The results at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level are presented in Table 1. The negative values of Ef imply that the two components of the GQD-PEB/PUB nanocomposites are adsorbed by each other.

The HOMO and LUMO, as well as the band gaps (Eg ), are determined to understand the electronic properties. The results in Table 1 demonstrate that the present Eg of GQD2 is 2.81 eV at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level, which is larger than the 1.96 eV [31] with Perdew-Zunger self-interaction correction [32] but smaller than the 4.68 eV with GW [31]. However, it is close to the theoretical result of Mahasin et al. [33], which suggests that the present theoretical level can give reliable results for the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites. The Eg s of all the nanocomposites are between 2.05 and 2.90 eV, which are a little smaller than those of the corresponding GQD except for the no change for the GQD3-PUB/PEB. Interestingly for GQD3-PEB, the changes of the HOMO and LUMO are just the same values, which results in the Eg remaining unchanged. Carefully examining Table 1, one can also find that the HOMOs, LUMOs, and Eg s of the nanocomposites are dominantly determined by GQD but not PUB/PEB, implying the GQDs play the main role in the electronic properties of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron transfer between the components of nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to inspect the feasibility of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites as the sensitizer of DSSC, we examine the HOMO and LUMO energies according to the requirements of the photoelectric anode and electrolyte. Since titanium dioxide (TiO2) and I− /I3 − are the most widely used and low-cost photoanode and electrolyte, respectively, we choose them as the example for discussing the role of sensitizer and the interaction between the anode/electrolyte and nanocomposite is omitted for the sake of brevity. In order to ensure that the electrons can efficiently transfer from LUMO to the conduction band of TiO2 (-4.3 eV) [34], the LUMO of the sensitizer should be higher than the conduction band of TiO2. Meanwhile, the sensitizer must be reduced by the electron contributed by the electron pair in the I− /I3 − electrolyte, which requires that the HOMO of the sensitizer is lower than the redox energy of the I− /I3 − (-4.8 eV) electrolyte [35]. Fortunately, all the LUMOs and HOMOs of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites can match the above conditions, as shown in Fig. 3 . In this sense, all the considered nanocomposites could have a chance to be used as a sensitizer of DSSC.

The charge density distributions of HOMO and LUMO for the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites are calculated to understand the spatial charge separation on the sensitizer. The results in Fig. 4 show that the charge density distributions of the GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites possess different characteristics. For the GQD1-PEB nanocomposites, HOMO is located at PEB but LUMO at GQD1; while for the GQD2-PEB one, HOMO is located at PEB but LUMO at GQD2. It means that the spatial charge separations in the two nanocomposites are substantial, which can effectively prevent the recombination of the photogenerated pair of electrons and holes. Therefore, the GQD1-PEB and GQD2-PEB are the preferable ones as the sensitizers of DSSC. Unfortunately, the HOMOs and LUMOs of the other GQD-PUB/PEB nanocomposites are located at the same component of GQD or PUB/PEB, which lead to that the recombination of the photogenerated pair being easy and the lifetime of the photogenerated electron reduces. The different characteristics of the HOMOs and LUMOs of the nanocomposites can also be understood through the energy alignments of the orbitals of the GQD and PUB/PEB. As shown in Table 1, the HOMO of PEB is higher than that of GQD1 and GQD2, while the LUMO is also higher than that of GQD1 and GQD2. These alternative levels of the frontier orbitals result in the charge distributions of HOMO and LUMO on different components of the nanocomposite. However, no such alignment exists in other structures. Thus, one can attribute the good spatial charge separation to the satisfactory energy alignment of PEB and GQD1/GQD2.

A semiclassical model of the Marcus theory of electron transfer [36] can be used to qualitatively understand the driving force of electron transfer. The recombination rate constant rate kab is evaluated by
(2)
k ab
=
V
ab
2
π
λ
k B
T
ħ 2
exp
-
( λ + Δ G )
2
4 λ
k B
T
where λ is the total recombination energy, and ΔG is the change of the Gibbs free energy during the electron transfer. The T, kB, and h represent the temperature, the Boltzmann constant, and the Planck constant, respectively. Vab
is the charge transfer integral between donor and acceptor states. According to Eq. (2), the electron-hole recombination rates for the same temperature are largely dependent on Vab
[37], while Vab is determined by the overlap of the wavefunctions of LUMO and HOMO [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 4., the overlap of the electron densities of the LUMOs and HOMOs of the GQD1-PEB and GQD2-PEB nanocomposites are trivial because they are located at the completely different components, which implies the Vab of the two nanocomposites are small. Then, the kab is small, implicating the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pair; the two nanocomposites are weak, which is beneficial for the photoelectronic performance as sensitizers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorptions of the GQD, PEB/PUB, and their nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The response to solar energy is mainly dependent on the optical absorption of the sensitizer in the UV and visible light ranges. Considering the popular DFT often suffers severe problems in describing the stability of CT states by using TDDFT, we assure the TDDFT liability by comparing the absorption peak of PEB/PUB by TDDFT with the corresponding experimental values [30]. Based on the TDDFT with B3LYP/6–31 + G(d), the oscillator strengths α of the obtained geometrical structures are calculated and shown in Fig. 5 . The present α peaks of PUB and PEB at 454 and 544 nm are in good agreement with the experimental 491 and 560 nm of Gaigalas et al [30], respectively. Therefore, the TDDFT can give acceptable optical absorptions for the present structures. To our surprise, the α of the nanocomposites are significantly enhanced in the visible light range in comparison with those of the separate GQD or PEB/PUB. Especially, the α of GQD2-PEB and GQD2-PUB are about 5 times that of PEB/PUB in the range from 400 to 600 nm, which suggests that the nanocomposites can well respond to sunlight and should be promising sensitizers of DSSC. As shown in Fig. 5, the largest molar absorption coefficient of 24.7 × 104 M−1cm−1 for GQD2-PEB is larger than 16.1 × 103, 15.0 × 103, 20.6 × 103, and 21.2 × 103 M−1cm−1 of phenoxazine-based organic dyes (POZ-2, POZ-3, POZ-4, POZ-5) [39].

The photoelectric conversion efficiency η is an important indicator of DSSC performance. Generally, η can be obtained from the following equation [40]:
(3)
η =
FF ·
J
sc
·
V
oc
P inc
where Pinc is the power of incident sunlight, Voc is the open circuit photovoltage, Jsc is the short circuit current density, and FF is the filling factor. Voc is defined as the maximum voltage generated by solar cells at zero current. It can be calculated by minimal electron injection from the LUMO of the sensitizer to the conduction band of photoanode [41]:
(4)
V
oc
=
E
L U M O
-
E
C B M
e

The short-circuit current density is associated with the light-harvesting efficiency (LHE), injection efficiency of electrons (Φinject ) in sensitizer excitation and charge collection efficiency (ηcollect ) of electrons injected at the optical anode, and can be obtained by[42]:
(5)
J
SC
= ∫ L H E
λ
Φ inject
η collect
d λ

At the maximum absorption wavelength (λ max), Jsc is proportional to LHE. Therefore, the sensitizers with higher LHE and Φinject values should have higher Jsc . LHE(λ) representations of light capture efficiency with a specific wavelength and can be calculated approximately by the following formula [43]:
(6)
L H E
( λ )
= 1 - 1
0
- f
where f is the oscillator strengths of the sensitizer. According to Beer's law, the oscillator strength correlated linearly with the molar extinction coefficient [44]. Φinject is determined by the changes in the free energy of the electrons which are able to successfully transfer to the surface of the electrode related to the driving force (ΔGinject ) of the electrons, which we can obtain by the following formula [45]:
(7)
Δ
G inject
=
E
ox
na
n
∗
-
E
CBM
(8)
E
ox
na
n
∗
=
E
OX
-
E
0 - 0
where ECBM represents the reduction potential of the guide band of the electrode.
E
ox
nan
∗
represents the photolytically excited state of the sensitizer, E0-0 is the vertical excitation energy between the ground and excited states, and EOX is the oxidation–reduction potential of the ground state of the sensitization.

The calculated
E
ox
na
n
∗
, ΔGinject , LHE, and Voc are collected in Table 2 . The value of η increases with Jsc and Voc , and the Jsc value of the sensitizer increases with the Φinject as determined by ΔGinject and LHE. The calculation results show that there is a significant increase in ΔGinject for all nanocomposites except GQD1-PEB, compared to PEB and PUB. This means that the excited electrons can be more spontaneously injected into the conduction band of TiO2. The Voc for all structures is a similar value, and only GQD3-PEB and GQD3-PUB have a lower Voc . The largest one is 1.74 V for GQD2-PEB. This value is apparently larger than 1.47 V of GOQD(18OH)-TPP reported by Gao et al. [23] in 2019. Thus, GQD2-PEB should be an excellent DSSC sensitizer candidate among all considered nanocomposites.

In summary, the geometrical structures and the electronic properties of six nanocomposites constructed with GQDs and PEB/PUB were studied at the B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) theoretical level, and the optical absorption properties were calculated by using TD-DFT. The stabilities of nanocomposites were confirmed with the harmonic frequencies and formation energy. The results demonstrate that the HOMO and LUMO levels of all nanocomposites can match the conditions of the typical DSSC with TiO2 and I− /I3 − electrolytes. The significant spatial charge separations and enhanced optical absorptions in the visible light range are observed for GQD1-PEB and GQD2-PEB. The examinations based on
E
ox
na
n
∗
, ΔGinject , LHE, Voc and the optical absorption indicate that GQD2-PEB could be achieved a higher photoelectric conversion efficiency among all the considered nanocomposites. These findings may provide helpful information for designing efficient sensitizers of DSSC based on the nanocomposites of GQD and dyes.

Feng-Lin Xing: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhi-Hong Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Supervision. Chuan-Lu Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mei-Shan Wang: Visualization. Xiao-Guang Ma: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant Nos. NSFC-11874192).
